candidate_problems.text: What are the non-circular criteria for determining when a target domain really has a load-bearing iterated-game structure rather than merely being redescribed that way by the analogy? The conjecture depends on distinguishing genuine hidden iteration from metaphorical relabeling, but it does not yet specify robust tests for recurrence, observability, payoff structure, agent boundaries, or strategic memory in messy real systems.

How can genuinely new knowledge from analogy be demarcated from knowledge already implicitly contained in background assumptions about the target? The conjecture claims novelty arises when imported iterated-game structure yields previously unknown consequences, but it leaves open how to measure epistemic gain, especially when the analogy partly works by reorganizing existing facts rather than adding new observations.

What is the scope limit of the 'hidden iteration' account of analogical reasoning: are there important knowledge-creating analogies whose power does not come from repeated interaction or game structure at all? If so, the conjecture may be too narrow; if not, it needs an explanation of why diverse successful analogies in mathematics, science, and philosophy can all be reduced to iterated strategic architecture.
